interest: The interest behind this argument is that of equity advocates, disadvantaged-pupil representatives, policy researchers, and reform movements who need the decision to rest on verifiable causal grounds rather than an untested transfer of the Portuguese precedent.
value: The tension is between equity (a reform that genuinely offsets early-selection harm) and administrative/political feasibility (adopting a resource-redistribution mechanism that preserves institutional continuity).
fear: The driving fear is that S4 will merely mask rather than dismantle sorting, permanently legitimizing early selection while relying on an unproven causal mechanism that leaves disadvantaged children worse off.
affected: families of children facing age-10 selection
assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that a mechanism unproven in any comparator with Hungary's early selection age (10-12) cannot be relied upon to work here, i.e., that absence of demonstrated offsetting is decision-critical rather than a gap that Hungarian evidence could fill.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself labels the offset-vs-mask distinction 'unverifiable ex ante' and concedes no comparator has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age, so the causal claim is genuinely unsettled rather than settled either way.
